data.title: Huron acquires global financial services consulting firm Treliant
data.description:
• Global professional services firm Huron (NASDAQ:HURN) announced it has acquired Treliant, an advisory and managed services partner to the financial services industry.
  • "This acquisition expands Huron’s Commercial segment offerings and enhances the firm’s ability to help financial institutions navigate today’s increasingly complex macroeconomic and regulatory environment," it said.
  • Treliant will be included in Huron’s Commercial operating segment within its consulting and managed services capabilities.
  • Terms of the acquisition were not disclosed.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Five9 GAAP EPS of $0.01 beats by $0.08, revenue of $283.27M beats by $8.09M
data.description:
• Five9 press release (NASDAQ:FIVN): Q2 GAAP EPS of $0.01 beats by $0.08.
  • Revenue of $283.27M (+12.4% Y/Y) beats by $8.09M.
  • For the full year 2025, Five9 expects to report:
    • Revenue in the range of $1.1435 to $1.1495 billion.
    • GAAP net income per share in the range of $0.23 to $0.30, assuming diluted shares outstanding of approximately 88.5 million.
    • Non-GAAP net income per share in the range of $2.86 to $2.90, assuming diluted shares outstanding of approximately 77.7 million.
  • For the third quarter of 2025, Five9 expects to report:
    • Revenue in the range of $283.0 to $286.0 million.
    • GAAP net income per share in the range of $0.06 to $0.12, assuming diluted shares outstanding of approximately 87.5 million.
    • Non-GAAP net income per share in the range of $0.72 to $0.74, assuming diluted shares outstanding of approximately 78.1 million.
  • Shares +5.69% AH.

data.title: GameStop shares fall for seven consecutive sessions
data.description:
GameStop (NYSE:GME) shares clocked seven straight sessions of losses on Thursday, as the stock closed 0.04% lower to $22.44.

The games and entertainment products company lost 6.3% in the preceding six sessions. Overall, the stock has lost 28% so far this year, compared to the over 8% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index (SP500).

GME is down 5% over the past one month. 

Most recently, Quantum BioPharma (QNTM), a biopharmaceutical company focused on treatments for neurodegenerative disorders, has acquired 2,000 shares of GameStop as a strategic investment to be held on its balance sheet.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, GME has a Hold rating with a score of 3.2 out of 5. The company received C+ in the prospect of profitability and a D- in the valuation factor.

Seeking Alpha analysts are also cautious and see the stock as a Hold.

 

“GME's aggressive pivot into the trading card market, including PSA partnerships and card grading, is a high-margin, high-growth opportunity being overlooked by the market,” pointed out a recent Seeking Alpha analysis.

data.title: AB InBev outlines 4%–8% EBITDA growth outlook with U.S. share gains and non-alcohol portfolio expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Michel Dimitrios Doukeris stated that "consistent execution of our strategy delivered another quarter of solid results, with EBITDA increasing by 6.5% and continued margin expansion." He highlighted "the performance of our premium brands and the strategic choices we made in revenue management drove an acceleration in our revenue per hectoliter growth, increasing by 4.9% versus last year."
  • Doukeris noted, "In the U.S., our portfolio is continuing to build momentum and gain share of the industry. We are continuing to increase our investments in our brands to fuel growth."
  • He reported, "Our non-alcohol beer portfolio continued to outperform globally, increasing revenues by 33%. The growth of BEES marketplace accelerated this quarter, increasing GMV by 63% versus last year to reach $785 million."
  • CFO Fernando Mommensohn Tennenbaum said, "Our EBITDA margins improved by 116 basis points this quarter with expansion in four of our five operating regions. We know that each quarter will be different, but we are confident that the combination of our leadership advantages, disciplined revenue management, continued premiumization and efficient operating model create an opportunity for further margin expansion over time."
  • Tennenbaum added, "We delivered underlying EPS of $0.98 per share, an 8.7% increase in U.S. dollars and a 17.4% increase in constant currency versus last year."

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed their 2025 guidance, with Tennenbaum stating, "Our results in the first half of the year, the resilience of our strategy and the strength of our megabrands all reinforce our confidence in our ability to deliver on our 2025 outlook of 4% to 8% EBITDA growth."
  • Doukeris stated, "Our first half performance and the strategic choices we have made position us well to deliver on our outlook for the year."
  • No new numerical guidance beyond the reiterated EBITDA growth range was provided this quarter.

Financial Results

  • Revenue increased by 3% this quarter versus last year, with EBITDA up by 6.5%.
  • Volumes declined by 1.9%, impacted primarily by performance in China and Brazil, but markets representing two-thirds of the business saw volume growth of 0.7%.
  • Underlying EPS was $0.98 per share, up 8.7% in U.S. dollars and 17.4% in constant currency.
  • Free cash flow increased by $0.5 billion versus last year.
  • Net debt-to-EBITDA ratio improved to 3.27x, compared to 3.42x year-over-year.
  • BEES marketplace GMV reached $785 million, a 63% increase year-on-year; digital platforms generated $134 million in revenue, a 6% rise.

Q&A

  • Mitchell John Collett, Deutsche Bank: Asked about the impact of volume declines and margin improvement sustainability. Doukeris responded that the company's global footprint "allows us to navigate different environments" and noted, "the long run does not change this industry, will continue to grow, is gaining share of growth, and we are confident on the business and the strategy that we are executing." Tennenbaum commented, "We don't provide margin outlook, but we said several times before that 2025 looks to be more like a normal year of cost inflation."
  • Robert Edward Ottenstein, Evercore ISI: Inquired about U.S. turnaround and lessons for other markets. Doukeris replied, "U.S. is being a topic in our calls... We've been talking a lot about this. So a long-term plan, a very consistent view on the category on our portfolio choices and the investments we made for the long term."
  • Jean-Olivier Nicolai, Goldman Sachs: Asked about Mexico and Brazil volume performance. Doukeris explained, "In Mexico... volumes during the quarter increased low single digits, outperformed the industry. Results were good top to bottom." He noted, "In Brazil, our revenue declined by 1.9%, impacted by volume performance."
  • Sanjeet Aujla, UBS: Queried on China and Brazil price dynamics. Doukeris stated, "The on-trade channel which is an important component... continues to be very weak. The growth that we see in the industry is more in the off-premise."
  • Andrea Pistacchi, Bank of America: Asked about U.S. profit levers amid industry decline and share buyback timing. Doukeris highlighted, "We are managing costs, improving productivity... those are very important levers together with mix and the growth of the Beyond Beer to continue to yield for us the top and the bottom line."
  • Edward Brampton Mundy, Jefferies: Discussed prospects for volume growth and category activation. Doukeris said, "Our developed markets... are all building on our full potential. We continue to believe that the long-term category opportunity in our business because of our footprint remains in place for us to deliver growth in the long term."
  • Laurence Bruce Whyatt, Barclays: Focused on U.S. mainstream brands and Europe volume growth. Doukeris responded, "We are more advanced with Ultra, more advanced with Busch Light while we continue to work on the other brands and make... the right choices in terms of allocation of capital and investments."
  • Chris Pitcher, Rothschild & Company: Asked about China consumption shifts and India investment. Doukeris affirmed, "We need to continue to protect the strength that we have on the on-trade... while with no doubt rewinding the whole business, not only the sales force for the off-premise is key."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about volume declines, margin sustainability, and the impact of macro headwinds, with a generally neutral to slightly negative tone, especially around China and Brazil.
  • Management maintained a confident and assertive tone in prepared remarks, using phrases such as "we remain confident on the footprint that we have and the advantage of this footprint globally."
  • During Q&A, management showed more nuance and defensiveness when pressed on volume growth and margin sustainability, evident in responses like "as we always say, this is a great footprint."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone shifted to slightly more pressing and management was more focused on defending strategic choices and explaining regional underperformance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, with reiteration of the 4%–8% EBITDA growth outlook.
  • Strategic focus continued on premiumization, non-alcohol portfolio expansion, and digital ecosystem growth.
  • This quarter, management acknowledged volume declines in China and Brazil, a shift from prior quarter's more upbeat volume commentary for Brazil.
  • U.S. market share and profitability momentum were further emphasized, along with innovation in non-alcohol and Beyond Beer categories.
  • Management tone was more defensive regarding volume trends, while analysts were more pointed in their questioning about regional headwinds and margin sustainability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited industry softness in China and Brazil as key challenges, with Doukeris noting, "our volumes underperformed with continued weakness in our regions and channels" in China and "the majority of our volume decline was driven by a soft industry" in Brazil.
  • Weather-driven impacts and consumer confidence pressures were highlighted as affecting several markets.
  • Tennenbaum addressed cost inflation and FX headwinds but stated, "we are confident that... efficient operating model create an opportunity for further margin expansion over time."
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned the sustainability of margin expansion and the prospects for volume growth in light of ongoing macro headwinds.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized continued confidence in delivering on the 2025 outlook of 4%–8% EBITDA growth, supported by strong performance in premium brands, margin expansion, and accelerated growth in digital and non-alcohol portfolios. The company acknowledged volume pressures in China and Brazil but highlighted ongoing momentum in the U.S. and other developed markets, strategic investments in megabrands, and increased free cash flow as positioning AB InBev to navigate market volatility and achieve its financial targets for the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Insight Enterprises outlines flat gross profit and $9.70–$10.10 EPS target for 2025 as AI-first strategy advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Insight Enterprises (NSIT) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Joyce A. Mullen, President and CEO, highlighted that "in Q2, we executed well and met our expectations as we navigated a challenging environment, primarily driven by partner program changes." Hardware revenue grew for the second consecutive quarter, and the company achieved record gross and operating margins for Q2, with total gross margin of 21.1% and adjusted earnings from operations margin of 6.2%.
  • Mullen stated that hardware revenue in North America increased 4%, commercial client revenue rose 8% for the fifth consecutive quarter, and underlying SaaS and Infrastructure as a Service business grew double digits. However, cloud gross profit declined 5% and Insight Core Services revenue was down 2% due to delays in services projects, especially among large enterprise clients.
  • Mullen emphasized a strategic pivot as the company adapts its ambition "to becoming not only the leading solutions integrator, but the leading AI-first solutions integrator," citing aggressive internal adoption of AI and integration of AI-first approaches in services. The company completed over 200 AI assessments with clients, more than quadrupling the previous quarter, and delivered productivity gains in software development.
  • Mullen announced that Gartner named Insight an emerging visionary in its innovation guide for generative AI consulting and implementation services, and the company was named a finalist for CRN's 2025 Best AI Solution Provider.
  • James A. Morgado, CFO, reported: "Net revenue was $2.1 billion, a decrease of 3% in U.S. dollars and 4% in constant currency. The decrease was driven by a 4% decline in product, primarily due to on-prem software, which declined 14%." He noted, "adjusted diluted earnings per share were $2.45, flat year-over-year in U.S. dollar terms and down 1% in constant currency."

Outlook

  • Morgado stated that for the full year, "we expect gross profit to be approximately flat from 2024 and that our gross margin will be approximately 20%. And our adjusted diluted earnings per share remains unchanged and will be between $9.70 to $10.10."
  • The company expects hardware gross profit to grow in the mid-single digits, core services to grow in the low single digits, and cloud performance to be flat to slightly down for the year. Demand from large enterprise clients is expected to modestly improve in the second half.
  • The guidance includes interest expense between $75 million to $80 million, an effective tax rate of 25% to 26%, capital expenditures of $30 million to $35 million, and an average share count for the full year of 32.4 million shares.

Financial Results

  • Net revenue was $2.1 billion, with a 3% decline. Hardware revenue increased 2%. Gross profit decreased 2%. Hardware gross profit was up 2%, while Insight Core Services gross profit was $78 million, a decrease of 3%. Cloud gross profit was $123 million, a decrease of 5%.
  • Adjusted SG&A declined 3%, resulting in adjusted EBITDA of $138 million, a decrease of 2%, while margin expanded to 6.6%.
  • Cash flow from operations was a use of $177 million for the quarter due to working capital, with full-year expectations unchanged at $300 million to $400 million.
  • The company repurchased approximately $76 million of shares in Q2, with $224 million remaining authorized for repurchase.

Q&A

  • Adam Tyler Tindle, Raymond James: Asked about the drivers behind the anticipated mid-single digit improvement in gross profit in the back half of the year and hardware demand trends. James A. Morgado responded that cloud landed in line with expectations, hardware did not see material pull-ins, and bookings support acceleration in hardware for the second half. OpEx performance enables the company to hold EPS guidance.
  • Tindle followed up on industry cost-cutting trends during a PC upcycle. Mullen replied, "we are really excited about the opportunities to implement basically AI technologies in almost every process...the productivity improvement is real...what that allows us to do is basically hold headcount flat while we increase -- some elements of our business are growing."
  • Joseph Lima Cardoso, JPMorgan: Inquired about delays in services projects and potential timing for reengagement. Mullen attributed delays to macro uncertainty and clients preserving capacity for AI investments, stating that "enterprises are keeping...some of the -- they're holding off on spending in more traditional areas to make sure they preserve capability to spend on AI."
  • Cardoso also asked about underlying cloud growth excluding program changes. Morgado stated, "the underlying cloud growth was approximately 17% year-over-year, very similar in Q2, a slight acceleration over that number."
  • Anthony Chester Lebiedzinski, Sidoti & Company: Asked about segment performance and public sector outlook. Mullen said, "public sector business is doing very well from a services point of view," and that most business is in SLED, less impacted by federal changes.
  • Vincent Colicchio, Barrington Research: Asked about labor strategy for AI and profitability initiatives. Mullen described a two-pronged approach—M&A/acquihires and internal upskilling—and confirmed ongoing profitability and pricing initiatives.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly cautious tone, pressing on the sustainability of hardware growth, industry-wide cost cutting, and timing for services recovery. There was evident concern about macro impacts and partner program headwinds.
  • Management displayed confidence in their AI-first strategy and cost discipline, using phrases such as "we are excited by the momentum in this space" and "we are well positioned." During Q&A, responses remained measured but optimistic, acknowledging challenges while emphasizing adaptability and execution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone remained cautious, but management's tone shifted to highlight greater confidence in AI-driven initiatives and the normalization of partner program disruptions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for adjusted diluted EPS remains unchanged at $9.70 to $10.10. The full-year gross profit outlook shifted from low-single-digit growth in Q1 to flat in Q2, reflecting more caution due to continued partner program impacts and muted services demand.
  • Strategic focus evolved from general solutions integration to an explicit "AI-first" positioning, with expanded internal and client-facing AI deployments.
  • Analysts continued to focus on macroeconomic headwinds, partner program changes, and the recovery timeline for services and hardware.
  • Management showed increased confidence in their AI capabilities, broader acknowledgement of cost control, and a more defined approach to addressing partner program normalization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty, tariffs, legislative impacts on supply chains, and interest rates as continuing to influence client investment decisions.
  • Delays in large enterprise services projects and partner program changes were noted as material headwinds, with management stating, "we are cautiously optimistic for the second half of the year as we navigate the macro factors that weigh on our client spending decisions."
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of hardware demand, timing for services recovery, and the impact of structural changes in partner programs.

Final Takeaway

Insight Enterprises reported resilient Q2 performance amid partner program changes and macro headwinds, maintaining its full-year adjusted EPS guidance of $9.70 to $10.10 while shifting to an AI-first strategic focus. Management highlighted productivity gains from internal AI adoption, stable commercial hardware growth, and expected normalization of partner-related headwinds by year-end. While challenges remain in services and cloud, the company emphasized disciplined expense management and confidence in leveraging its AI capabilities to drive future growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: UDR raises 2025 FFOA per share guidance and targets 2% blended lease rate growth supported by strong demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: UDR, Inc. (UDR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Chairman and CEO Thomas W. Toomey described the first half of 2025 as benefiting from “employment and income growth exceeding consensus expectations, relative affordability squarely in the favor of apartments, and new supply pressures waning.” He reported “record high absorption” and credited UDR’s strategies for “accelerating pricing power, higher resident retention, lower concessions and strong expense control.” Toomey announced, “our second quarter and first half same-store revenue, expense and NOI growth all exceeded our initial guidance provided back in February.”
  • Toomey revealed a strategic focus on leveraging UDR’s customer experience project, innovation initiatives leading to “high single-digit growth from rentable items,” and ongoing capital deployment into development, redevelopment, and preferred equity investments, all underpinned by an “investment-grade balance sheet with substantial liquidity.”
  • Toomey introduced Dave Bragg as the new Chief Financial Officer, highlighting his “wealth of industry experience and executive leadership.”
  • COO Michael D. Lacy detailed that “second quarter year-over-year same-store revenue and NOI growth of 2.5% and 2.9%, respectively, were better than expected,” with blended lease rate growth of 2.8%, renewal rate growth of 5%, and occupancy averaging 96.9%.
  • Lacy reported that “income growth from rentable items was 10%, driven by continued innovation.”
  • CFO Joseph D. Fisher stated, “Our second quarter FFO as adjusted per share of $0.64 exceeded the high end of our previously provided guidance. The $0.03 or 5% sequential FFOA per share increase was driven by a $0.02 increase from same-store NOI, with contributions from both higher-than-expected revenue growth and lower-than-expected expense growth, and a $0.01 contribution from the collection of previously unaccrued interest related to one of our former debt and preferred equity investments.”

Outlook

  • Fisher raised UDR’s full year 2025 FFOA per share guidance range to $2.49 to $2.55, stating, “The $2.52 midpoint represents a $0.02 per share or approximately 1% improvement compared to our prior guidance.” The third quarter FFOA per share guidance range is $0.62 to $0.64.
  • Lacy shared, “we raised our midpoint by 25 basis points, resulting in a new range of 1.75% to 3.25%” for same-store revenue growth, with a full-year forecast for blended lease rate growth of approximately 2%.
  • Lacy noted that “our expectations for blended lease rate growth in the second half of the year have come down versus the prior guidance, but outperformance in the first half of the year enables us to maintain the contribution to 2025 same-store revenue growth.”
  • Same-store expense growth midpoint was lowered by 50 basis points to 3%.

Financial Results

  • Fisher highlighted, “Our second quarter FFO as adjusted per share of $0.64 exceeded the high end of our previously provided guidance.”
  • Lacy stated, “year-over-year same-store expense growth of only 1.7% in the second quarter came in much better than expectations,” mainly due to savings on real estate taxes and insurance.
  • UDR reported more than $1.1 billion of liquidity as of June 30, with debt to enterprise value at 28% and net debt-to-EBITDAre at 5.5x.
  • Fisher discussed notable transactions, including the acquisition and consolidation of the Broadridge apartment community in Philadelphia and receiving nearly $55 million in proceeds from a preferred equity investment payoff in New York.

Q&A

  • Nicholas Gregory Joseph, Citigroup: Asked about confidence in achieving rent growth in the second half given weaker peak leasing season. Lacy explained, “the guidance raise has everything to do with the execution of everything we've done up until this point of the year… our revenue growth at this point in the year is about 80% baked.” He also noted renewal growth expectations for the rest of the year would be “in that 4% to 4.5% range.”
  • Joseph followed up about whether rent growth differences across regions would narrow. Lacy responded, “During the first quarter, the spread between the coastal markets and what we're experiencing in the Sunbelt was right around 450 basis points... Moving forward, my expectation is back half coast could come down a little bit, and the Sunbelt is still showing some signs of some positive momentum.”
  • James Colin Feldman, Wells Fargo: Inquired about which markets saw expectation changes and about Los Angeles trends. Lacy said the West Coast “has done better than we would have expected year-to-date,” while Sunbelt lagged but is “expected to improve on a quarter-over-quarter basis but maybe not to the level we would have expected at the beginning of the year.” On Los Angeles, he stated, “for us, during the quarter, we averaged 96% occupancy. Concessions were right around 1 week and blends were between 1% to 1.5% growth.”
  • Sanketkumar Rajeshbhai Agrawal, Evercore: Asked about external growth opportunities and funding. Fisher described a “relatively healthy transaction market” and a “capital balanced approach,” with funding strategies including refinancing and continued use of commercial paper.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the sustainability of growth, regional performance, and expense controls, with a generally neutral to slightly positive tone. Questions were detailed and pressed for clarity around assumptions for guidance and regional dynamics.
  • Management’s tone was confident during prepared remarks, with Toomey and Lacy repeatedly emphasizing execution and outperformance, and Fisher describing a “pretty healthy transaction market.” There was slight caution in discussing Sunbelt recovery and expense trends.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts remained focused on similar topics but pressed more about the back half’s risks and regional variability. Management’s tone was more assertive in describing achieved results and future confidence, supported by “record high absorption” and upgraded guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was raised in Q2, in contrast to Q1 when management reaffirmed guidance and cited only four months of results. The Q2 call highlighted exceeding initial expectations for same-store revenue, expense, and NOI growth.
  • Strategic focus continues on innovation and customer experience, but Q2 included more concrete figures on blended lease rate growth, occupancy rates, and expense savings.
  • Analysts’ questions in Q2 focused more on granular regional performance and operational levers, while Q1 centered on overall macro risks and supply/demand dynamics.
  • Management’s tone in Q2 was notably more confident, with repeated references to outperformance and upwardly revised guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing supply pressures in the Sunbelt and the need to monitor market-specific trends, particularly as supply decreases and job growth forecasts remain strong.
  • Lacy noted that “expectations now are it's going to continue to improve on a quarter-over-quarter basis but maybe not to the level we would have expected at the beginning of the year” for the Sunbelt.
  • Expense pressures, particularly noncontrollable items, were discussed, though Lacy expects controllable expenses to moderate in the second half.
  • Fisher described lessons learned from recent development and preferred equity investments, emphasizing tighter scenario analysis, shorter extension options, and a shift toward recapitalizations with safer underwriting.

Final Takeaway

Management reported record absorption and strong demand fundamentals in the first half of 2025, driving outperformance in revenue, NOI, and expense control. UDR raised its full-year FFOA per share and same-store revenue growth guidance, underpinned by innovation, high retention, and a disciplined capital approach. While Sunbelt markets face lingering supply headwinds, management expressed confidence in continued momentum and capital flexibility to support long-term growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Commerce rebrand signals shift to AI-driven agentic commerce and targets $355M ARR
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Commerce (formerly BigCommerce) (BIGC) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Travis Hess opened by highlighting a pivotal quarter marked by a corporate rebrand to Commerce, stating, "Q2 represented solid progress for the business in a number of areas. We delivered non-GAAP operating income of nearly $4.8 million, a 335 basis point margin improvement year-over-year. Annual revenue run-rate or ARR reached nearly $355 million, a year-over-year improvement of 3%. Revenue reached $84.4 million in the quarter, growing 3% year-over-year, and operating cash flow came in at approximately $13.6 million, an improvement of nearly $2 million year-over-year. Both revenue and non-GAAP operating income exceeded the high side of our guidance range."
  • Hess detailed the company's transition: "Our transformation phase is over. We have moved fully into execution and growth, and we are proud to reintroduce our company as Commerce. This rebrand as Commerce marks the culmination of a year spent rebuilding the company for where the commerce industry is going for the agentic future."
  • Partnerships and product innovation were emphasized, with Hess noting, "We've launched our partnership with Perplexity, a leading AI answer engine... Our expanded relationship with Google Cloud is helping merchants stand out across sales channels with AI-enriched product data... Today, we also announced a new partnership with PROS, a market leader in AI-driven pricing optimization and configure-price-quoting."
  • Hess cited new customer wins including Revelyst, URBN, Tapestry, and Dell Technologies leveraging Commerce’s data integrations.
  • CFO Daniel Lentz stated, "Commerce currently serves over 5,800 enterprise accounts and tens of thousands of small businesses. ARR reached nearly $355 million at quarter-end, a 3% increase year-over-year, while average revenue per enterprise account rose to $46,403, a 9% increase year-over-year. We delivered $84.4 million in revenue in the quarter, up over 3% year-over-year, and non-GAAP operating income of $4.8 million."
  • Lentz explained, "Non-GAAP gross margin strengthened to 80%, up 280 basis points year-over-year, while non-GAAP operating income margin finished Q2 at 6%, improving 335 basis points from Q2 2024 and 1,013 basis points from Q2 2023. We closed Q2 2025 with a solid balance sheet, including $136 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities."

Outlook

  • Lentz provided guidance: "For Q3, we expect revenue between $85 million and $87 million and non-GAAP operating income between $2.3 million and $3.3 million. For the full year 2025, we expect revenue between $339.6 million and $346.6 million and non-GAAP operating income between $19 million and $25 million."
  • Lentz added, "We are on track to release an integrated, self-serve version of Feedonomics within the BigCommerce control panel by this holiday season. Our self-serve version of Makeswift within the BigCommerce platform and our branded payments solution are also on track to be released in the front half of 2026."

Financial Results

  • Commerce reported $84.4 million in revenue for Q2 2025, a 3% year-over-year increase, and non-GAAP operating income of $4.8 million.
  • Average revenue per enterprise account increased to $46,403, up 9% year-over-year.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin was 80%, up 280 basis points year-over-year.
  • Operating cash flow reached approximately $14 million, up $2 million from Q2 2024.
  • Net debt position reduced to $18 million, a 73% decrease year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Raimo Lenschow, Barclays, asked about the impact of tariffs and field trends. Hess responded, "We're not seeing a lot of impact thus far from tariffs... We're continuing to see success, obviously, in demand, particularly on the B2B side."
  • Lenschow inquired about rebranding disruption. Lentz answered, "We are not rebranding the individual products themselves... I don't think that it's really going to affect deal volume or pipeline build because, again, this is not impacting the branding of the individual products themselves."
  • Hoi-Fung Wong, Oppenheimer, pressed on product portfolio readiness post-rebrand. Hess stated, "I would expect to see material signs of optimism and signs of growth that would most notably turn into revenue probably more so in the early part of next year materially versus second half of this year."
  • Natalie Howe, Bank of America, asked about B2B adoption and payments. Hess highlighted momentum and the PROS partnership for complex CPQ needs. Lentz described the branded payment solution as "really focused on small businesses and mid-market customers."
  • David Hynes, Canaccord, questioned Commerce’s positioning as platform vs. data layer. Hess emphasized both, signaling flexibility and expanded TAM; Lentz noted, "I would anticipate in the future that the Feedonomics product would grow at a disproportionate rate relative to the platform product."
  • Jeffrey Parker Lane, Stifel, discussed sales pipeline and margin expansion. Lentz said, "Pipeline build has been healthy... win rates improving are definitely encouraging," and "we are prioritizing growth acceleration."
  • Madison Taylor Schrage, KeyBanc, asked about B2B net adds and ARPA. Hess stated, "A disproportionate amount of net new bookings has been oriented to B2B..." Lentz added, "We've done a better job with pricing discipline... winning larger and more complex customers."
  • Brian Peterson, Raymond James, inquired about AI impacts. Hess explained, "The demand in market right now, particularly with B2C merchants... is around discoverability."
  • Gabriela Borges, Goldman Sachs, focused on agentic search. Hess confirmed, "The answer is yes, substantially," regarding customers seeing negative impact from agentic search and adopting Commerce solutions.
  • Christopher Louis Kuntarich, UBS, asked about B2C discovery trends. Hess noted, "The general percentage that I've heard in multiple corners is 20% drop-off in organic search for a lot of brands."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions about macro headwinds, rebranding disruption, B2B growth, agentic commerce, and the impact of AI on sales cycles, with a tone that was largely neutral but at times probing about risks and near-term visibility.
  • Management's tone was confident in prepared remarks, shifting to detailed and occasionally cautious explanations in Q&A, especially around the pace of market transformation and customer adoption. Hess stated, "I am encouraged by the progress that I see, and I am confident we can build on our momentum."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a similar mix of caution and optimism, with more focus this quarter on agentic commerce and the company's rebranding.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The rebranding to Commerce and explicit pivot to AI-driven agentic commerce marks a significant strategic shift from Q1's focus on operational transformation and leadership changes.
  • Q2 guidance targets revenue between $85 million and $87 million, compared to Q1 guidance of $82.5 million to $83.5 million, showing a slight increase in expectations.
  • Average revenue per enterprise account increased from just over $45,000 in Q1 to $46,403 in Q2.
  • Management's confidence is bolstered by new partnerships and product launches, while analysts' focus has shifted from sales force ramp and pipeline to the implications of AI and answer engines on business performance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariff-related uncertainty persists, though management reports little direct impact so far.
  • The pace of adoption for agentic commerce and the efficacy of new partnerships remain to be seen, with Hess indicating, "I would expect to see material signs of optimism and signs of growth... probably more so in the early part of next year materially versus second half of this year."
  • Ongoing need to improve sales and marketing efficiency.
  • Potential for revenue mix shift as Feedonomics growth may outpace the core platform.

Final Takeaway

Commerce delivered a solid Q2 2025 with revenue and non-GAAP operating income exceeding guidance, highlighted by a strategic rebrand that positions the company at the forefront of AI-driven agentic commerce. Management signaled confidence in the company's pipeline, new partnerships, and product innovation, emphasizing readiness to capitalize on the evolving landscape of AI-powered shopping and shifting consumer behavior. Investors should note the increased focus on B2B growth, data optimization, and partnerships, as well as management's clear intent to drive higher-value customer wins and margin improvement as the company transitions from transformation to execution.
